sentences.text: Ah, yeah.
They brought a convoy about-- only-- only men, Polish Jew, Polish-Jewish people.
I don't know from-- from where, from Warsaw or from Cracow.
I-- I believe.
Maybe from prison, Pawiak, from Warsaw.
That familiar to you, Pawiak?
Or maybe from [GERMAN], that's the Gestapo prisoner.
And they brought to Auschwitz, told them undress, naked.
There come a convoy, soldier with the rifle.
He told them, those people, to run in different direction.
And the-- there, SS shoot them.
Yeah.
Yeah, by the-- that's why-- that's why the whole reason to ask for them not to look, lay down on the-- on the floor.
But I was looking.
I see what happened.
Oh, many times.
And there was also-- made it from Polish-Jewish, they're calling [GERMAN], short.
[GERMAN], the German call it, [GERMAN].
[NON-ENGLISH]-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] how do you call it?
[NON-ENGLISH]?
There was-- they put on the-- on the-- they used to leave those people, Polish-Jewish people, live in the block 11.
And they'd been working very hard and very-- treated very bad.
Many of them been killed on the work, on the job.
Many people been killed in the block 11, or many people had been executed.
In Auschwitz.
And the time, it's-- I was not even thinking.
You know, I was live a-- I was-- I feel like-- like I'm an animal, not human.
[CRYING]
No.
I was just-- when I see those people, when they went to Auschwitz, I say, what-- I say to myself, what kind of people there are, you see?
That, to me, I said, it looks like a zoo.
Mhm.
Yes.
I-- because I-- after I-- I escape from commando to commando, from job to job.
Then when they caught me, they whip me.
Well, I'm-- I'm in Poland because the job was too-- too hard for me.
[LAUGHS]
So I try-- I try to do anything to-- to work easy.
But there was no easy.
And they whipped you?
Yeah.
My hands, you can see.
I was sick lately.
There were still-- there was, I believe, 1941 or '42, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they put me on the river.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because I-- I-- I-- change the time-- I jumped from-- there was impossible to describe everything already-- already been done to people, too, done to me.
Uh-huh.
[INAUDIBLE] I-- I-- I want to be-- say about my dear beautiful [INAUDIBLE], woman.
And the bullet holes are through the-- through the-- four?
I believe four trucks.
SS brought inside to the Auschwitz, to the block 11, where they were being executed, Polish women, Polish-Jewish women, Polish-- 18, 20 years.
Yeah, three, I believe four or six trucks, loaded.
And when trucks went through-- through the Auschwitz, for the execution.
On the living block.
They call the living block.
No, that was in Auschwitz.
Yeah, yeah.
They brought-- the convoy brought in inside, in Auschwitz.
Yes Because you know, we've been working, make believe, you know, not to look at that but [INAUDIBLE].
Mostly.
Every day, brought fresh transport, segregated, on the front of the gas chamber crematorium.
Yeah.
I believe also younger people, put to-- because the German knew they were labor work.
All the people, mostly going to the gas chamber or executed.
Younger people, they're keeping in a-- in a camp for-- for their working.
I believe, there was only one crematorium, I believe, in it--
--yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
To work.
The other people, I believe they-- they put in the gas chamber.
Yeah, yes.
I'm sure there were already built.
To-- to build this gas chamber, there were everybody, prisoner, like me, which I [INAUDIBLE].
But it's everybody, Polish-Jewish, Polish people, German, German prisoner.
Because there was many German prisoners in the camp too.
Yes.
I believe there was four in-- in Auschwitz.
Two in Birkenau.
No.
I-- we been-- I-- I-- we been in the barracks.
I've been working in these barracks.
Yeah, in Birkenau.
1940-- I believe I was transferred from Auschwitz to Birkenau, I believe 1942.
Yes.
I work in the [GERMAN], chopping the wood.
Which also is the work, we're sawing with the saw.
That's why I-- they-- they-- well, now I was stuck in with a friend of mine, when they caught me.
And then that's why I got 50 whips.
And that's 100 whips because two-- two whips, they counted as one.
So after appell, everybody, they counted the [GERMAN].
Each-- each block, 1,050-- 1,200 people.
They belong one SS man, they're calling-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You have to be able to say-- one like a-- like an Oberscharfuhrer.
[INAUDIBLE] black in-- in appell.
Appell, that mean counted how many people.
Than after appell, they call my number for the-- for the punishment, for the whips.
Very easy, with the number.
And the-- and the--
I believe one of the SS men.
I-- I-- SS men-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] could be Oberscharfuhrer, the Sarge.
Or [GERMAN], [GERMAN].
That's mean like a colonel.
Oh, just because I was talking to a friend of mine on the work.
Yeah.
After appell they call my number.
So I have this-- I-- I go forward.
So there was a-- the whips was made from a bull.
You know, they were the same, with the two, three strokes to the skin, broken.
Then I have to count it.
[GERMAN], two whips for [GERMAN].
[GERMAN], [GERMAN].
If you make me [INAUDIBLE].
Start, yeah.
So I-- I-- I was very strong.
But many people got whips too, 20.
And they bled throughout-- from out here.
And the person died.
I was so strong.
It was unbelievable.
Like-- like-- like 100.
Because two-- two whips, they were counted as one.
Told me 50 whips.
If you counted-- and--
So-- like correctly, that's it, 100 whips.
No.
I've been-- and the next day I-- I went to work.
No-- no forgettings.
I know the skin was black.
And no, there's nothing, nothing.
No-- no care.
And so I been struck a couple times by the-- like yesterday, [INAUDIBLE], which my ear is almost broken.
But I got good-- good sounds.
Only I have noise in my head.
But I got excellent sounds.
I hear.
Yeah.
I got broken drum from-- from the hitting.
Then all day, 24 hours, I got noise like a motor.
Like you know while I talk, I hear the noise.
I-- I get-- I get used to it.
No, I got good hearing.
I don't know, a miracle.
Ah.
I was sick, I believe, 1942, in Auschwitz.
Like, there was evening appell.
All [INAUDIBLE] men forward, after-- after appell.
So-- so-- ever-- whoever sick, or got [INAUDIBLE] on their foot, forward the-- for the checking.
So the-- my captain-- like, the doctor.
They ask me in Polish, do you have temperature?
You have a-- they say Polish, [SPEAKING POLISH].
Show me your tongue.
I mean, [SPEAKING POLISH].
They speak beautiful Polish.
Kept on like a-- a-- a doctor.
Then they left, left me to [INAUDIBLE] Hospital.
And many people wants to go too, into [INAUDIBLE], healthy people.
All right, hold them.
And so soon, eight SS truck-- army truck come in.
And they get loaded in trucks, straight to crematorium, to gas chamber.
No.
Miracle.
So I was very sick.
I got a thing I'm going to show you.
Mhm.
I had [INAUDIBLE] on both legs.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And I'm strong.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Even every morning I made it for a little push up, even today.
Yeah.
Uh-huh.
Yes.
Uh-huh.
So next morning, the same day I was worried-- same-- same-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --date, same night, I was sick.
I had water in-- I believe, in the right side.
Yes.
I had-- next morning, when I come in, they-- I don't know which doctor, or Dr-- Dr.
Mengele, or the other doctor, two of them took me to the laboratorium next-- next day from-- from the [INAUDIBLE].
They drain the blood from my brain.
I was passed out.
I was-- I know that both of them-- there was like-- like here, two-story building.
The doctor, Mengele, or the other doctor, took me on the arm, both of them.
I would have to find out in Auschwitz.
Then they-- in a few second, I was dead.
They brought me into Auschwitz.
And the Dr.
Mengele or the other doctor kept on-- told the-- the doctor prisoner.
Because Doctor told me after that he was cured.
Because they told me I was very lucky.
And the doctor, the prisoner told me, you are very lucky.
You know the doctor-- they kept on-- the doctor told us if I'm [INAUDIBLE], we-- we're going to [INAUDIBLE] for you.
Yeah.
I was-- I had very high fever.
They tied me to the bed.
I broke every piece of a blanket.
I tried to jump through the window.
No, no, I was a-- a high temperature.
I was like, you know, after that operation.
No.
Those-- no, no, it was a normal hospital.
Yeah.
But they-- that's-- like especially, I don't know why they was interested about me.
Those-- those two and [INAUDIBLE] and a doctor, Dr.
Mengele, or I don't know which-- which one.
And they--
Was a top brass SS man, with a rank, medallion, nice, handsome.
Young man.
That I must say, I was young too.
Mengele was young too.
I have-- I have his picture in the book.
He was a--
I believe yes, in the fall, I believe, I'm sure.
Yeah, yeah.
Because that was raining, snow, yes.
Yes.
No, they-- they call me to the room.
I woke up.
And they took me on the arm, those two doctor, to them labratorium.
And there was especially that barrack for the-- for the-- for the-- for the doctor, the SS.
No.
I pass out.
I don't know, nothing.
I was dead.
I was completely-- I--
And I-- I had fever after that when--
Yeah, yeah.
I guess-- I guess somewhere-- somewhere right here, they-- they cut me here, somewhere.
Just once.
Yeah. the doctor told me I had like a stroke blood flooded my brain.
Yeah.
Then after, that's why the doctor, the SS told the-- doctor prisoners, if I'm going to [? died, ?] all of you are going to [? died. ?] So take care of-- let me take care of him.
And that's me.
Yeah.
Then after that, they-- they ship out from Auschwitz to Birkenau.
A couple-- couple weeks later.
Gee, I-- I really-- I guess I went normal, to the barrack.
There was a main room, like this one.
There was table.
And-- and they just didn't-- didn't talk to me.
They-- they talk between themselves.
In German.
They knew already, what to do.
Full of medicines, full of--
Yes, yes.
Yes, and about the room, mirrors.
Nobody but me.
They took me.
Doctor SS with his-- they're calling [GERMAN].
And because I was so weak I could not walk they took me on-- on the arm.
I'm the only one.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
I-- you know, I don't know nothing.
Because I was like a dead.
They did something to me in the-- in the doctor office.
Then I don't know how, I find myself in a-- in a bed.
Yeah.
I guess so.
I got a good memory, a good everything, a good spirit.
Till now, I believe in God.
I don't think so.
So far, till now, I'm just myself.
I'm normal person.
So maybe try to did some to me.
And thank God, I'm the miracle one.
No.
Then after they ship out to finish us in Birkenau.
Because when-- when they give us now a tattoo number.
Yeah.
Yeah.
In-- in Birkenau, they give us already.
Yes.
I believe we had been in-- that-- that's not far away, about maybe five kilometer, maybe three kilometer.
Well, I believe we walk.
Oh-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] maybe 10,000, maybe more.
Yeah.
Yes, many people.
They were so disorganized.
They were-- the barracks was not ready.
There-- there was no place to sleep.
There were-- that was like chaos.
In different barracks.
Because there was straw with the-- with the-- as you call it, mattress, what they fill it with straw.
But also on the pile.
So we sleep where-- where-- whatever we can.
Because it was-- for a couple days, it was disorganized.
The SS don't know what to do.
And that was unbelievable.
So these were 1,000 people now, in Birkenau.
Yes.
No, no.
Yes.
After, when the Birkenau build it, they come equal.
Mostly transport from Europe come to [INAUDIBLE].
[INAUDIBLE] was very famous, two crematorium.
No, not-- maybe couple-- couple days or couple-- couple weeks.
OK, now there was each number, 3/4 needle.
And they press it through the skin.
You can hear when the-- when the skin was like, pop, pops.
So many Russian soldier, prisoner been also tattooed.
22, 54, [GERMAN].
German too, I will never forget it.
Because I were what the German call.
[GERMAN], what's your name?
Number?
[SPEAKING GERMAN].
OK, there was three-- three or four barrack where-- that-- that was in the fall, in-- in Birkenau.
They closed down-- in the evening, barracks.
I believe that was wintertime, because the frosty ground, in the snow on the ground, we have to take bath.
And that-- this barrel was deep in the ground.
So the time I had big-- almost like I could see my bone.
I have to jump into the-- into the-- the barrel and wash myself.
And so the barracks was closed and nobody-- and nobody go into the barracks.
And in the morning, half of those people been froze dead.
You survived it.
What was-- do you have-- did you have a number of the barrack in-- in Birkenau?
Do you remember?
I-- really, I don't remember.
That was not by number.
But I don't remember.
I believe that we been together with the Polish-Jewish, you know, Polish.
Yeah.
Russian soldiers at war.
That's especially Russian-- Russian soldiers had number with the Polish people.
I believe at start of the war.
Only men, yes.
Yes, I saw-- I been-- after change commando, I'd been working as a [GERMAN].
Dug-- That mean repairing roof, put new roof on the barracks.
In the Birkenau.
We went to the gypsy barracks.
We went to the-- woman barracks, separated from the gypsy.
Gypsy used to live in the Birkenau, whole family, with the children.
With like 10, 12, 15 people.
With the family, yes.
In the beginning, the German deliver them for the children, milk and white bread, by purpose.
In a couple days, everybody go to gas chamber.
Yeah.
No.
But we been talking about gas chamber, crematorium.
So to me, they was scared by even looking.
At the nighttime, Birkenau, there was a-- a smoke come from a chimney with-- with the-- white [INAUDIBLE] spark.
And-- and the smell, there was an odor from the-- from the bone.
So it was terrible.
Yeah.
Daytime, nighttime.
That crematorium was working 24 hours.
And-- I-- I spoke with the-- I believe it was not allowed, Polish-Jewish who work in it.
They're calling sonderkommando.
That was sad.
They knew what-- what going to happen to them.
[CRYING]
Because they knew.
After a couple months I say, we have to go.
They're going to pick up fresh people.
Nice-looking people, young.
I don't think so.
But they were some very young, nice people.
Kanada, and there was people who pick up-- yes, leave the jewelry, money on the side, the clothes on the other side.
And that's called Kanada.
In fact, in Kanada, they're calling.
In Kanada, that was people will-- will pick-- pick up the clothes and jewelry, separate it.
And I mean, many, many of them-- they-- they stole the jewelry, mostly the SS.
The black market was in Auschwitz, I'll tell you true.
SS bring them whiskey, cold cuts, salami, and things like that.
And they try to buy the jewelry, gold coins from-- from Polish prisoner in Auschwitz.
Yeah.
Maybe Birkenau.
I-- but there was-- the Kanada was in Birkenau, in Auschwitz too.
That this is the reason why they call-- called it Kanada.
Because Kanada, there was everything, what you needed.
What you collected, like a--
Yes.
Gold, diamond, rubies, everything, everything.
Mostly the people brought from France, from also, very rich people.
And they have to leave everything.
And-- and they get either way, gas chamber or going to-- to concentration camp, to Auschwitz.
No.
I have no chance about here, about the Kanada, yes.
That's the Kanada.
Till now that Kanada, that's-- that's a goodie.
No, no.
But I know the people who work in-- in fact, in Kanada.
People who work, in fact, in Kanada, people who give a new uniform for us, they-- they change the clothes maybe every couple weeks, fresh one.
And Given a shirt.
And that's it.
When I was sick after, I went to-- they're calling [INAUDIBLE].
They're peeling potatoes.
